title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
This document maps how competency-based selection works in the Irish public
service, an area the knowledge base previously left blank despite Ireland being
an English-language EU member state that publishes its frameworks openly. It is
the first Ireland-specific document in the knowledge base and a deepening child
of `eu-frameworks-foundation`, which covers only the EU institutions and EPSO.

The central body is the **Public Appointments Service (PAS)**, which trades as
**publicjobs** and is the centralised recruiter for the Irish Civil Service and
much of the wider public service [1] [8]. The single most important currency
fact is that Ireland has **moved from a competency model to a "Capability
Framework"**: PAS designed a new Civil Service Capability Framework, launched in
**February 2024**, spanning every grade from Clerical Officer to Principal
Officer [3] [4] [5] [16]. It does not simply rename the old competencies — it widens the
lens to seven "capability dimensions" (behaviours, skills, strengths, knowledge,
values, motivation and interests) organised under four Capabilities [1] [4].
Crucially, **both systems are live**: PAS states plainly that "some roles may
utilise the Civil Service Competency Model and others may rely on the new
Capability Framework", and candidates are told in advance which applies [5].

Interviews are structured and evidence-based. PAS runs competency-based and
capability-based interviews (increasingly by automated or live video), and — a
gift for this knowledge base — **publishes its own example questions** for both
formats [5]. Selection is governed by the statutory regulator, the **Commission
for Public Service Appointments (CPSA)**, whose Code of Practice sets six
recruitment principles (probity, merit, best practice, consistency,
transparency, equality/diversity/inclusion) and mandates review and complaint
routes [9] [10] [14].

The document is rated **Medium** confidence. The core facts rest on primary PAS
(publicjobs), gov.ie, CPSA, Garda and HSE documents that were opened and read
directly. The
rating is held at Medium because the UK/EPSO comparison table is this knowledge
base's own labelled analysis (no publisher states it), and because some
sector-body detail — notably the specific competencies used in the Garda
interview — is issued to candidates privately and is a **verified negative** in
the public record rather than a documented fact.
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
- The **Public Appointments Service (PAS / publicjobs)** is the centralised,
  independent recruiter for the Irish Civil Service and public service, and runs
  open competitions through stages: application, shortlisting, online tests, and
  interview [5] [8]. *(documented)*
- Ireland has a **new Civil Service Capability Framework, launched February
  2024**, covering Clerical Officer up to Principal Officer; it is broader than
  the old competency model, assessing seven capability dimensions — behaviours,
  skills, strengths, knowledge, values, motivation and interests. *(documented)*
  [1] [3] [4] [16]
- The **legacy Civil Service Competency Model has not been abolished** — PAS
  confirms some roles still use it and tells candidates in advance which model
  applies. Do not assume the Capability Framework governs your competition. [5]
  *(documented)*
- The four Capabilities are **Building Future Readiness, Evidence Informed
  Delivery, Leading and Empowering, and Communicating and Collaborating**, with
  sub-dimensions that grow with grade (Clerical Officer has no "Leading"
  capability; senior grades add strategy/reform and influencing sub-dimensions).
  *(documented)* [1] [2] [3]
- PAS **publishes example interview questions** for both competency-based and
  capability-based interviews, and recommends the **STAR** structure for
  competency answers. *(documented)* [5]
- **Sector bodies run their own competency selection**: An Garda Síochána (a
  publicjobs-run competency-based interview plus a role-play), the HSE
  (competency-based application and interview), and local authorities (a
  distinct competency set) each publish candidate guidance. *(documented)*
  [11] [12] [13]
- Selection is regulated by statute: the **Public Service Management
  (Recruitment and Appointments) Act 2004** created the CPSA, whose **Code of
  Practice** sets six recruitment principles and requires review/complaint
  procedures. *(documented)* [9] [10] [14]
- The comparison of Irish capabilities with UK Civil Service Behaviours and EPSO
  competencies in this document is **the knowledge base's own analysis**, not a
  published concordance. *(guidance)*
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
This document answers, for both audiences:

- **Candidates** targeting an Irish public-service competition (Civil Service,
  An Garda Síochána, HSE, local authority): which framework governs the role,
  what the named competencies/capabilities are, how the competition and
  interview are structured, and what PAS itself tells candidates to do.
- **Interviewers and HR** in or around the Irish public service, and readers
  comparing systems across markets: what the official framework and process
  documents actually say, how PAS structures and governs selection, and where
  the Irish scheme sits relative to the UK Civil Service and EPSO.

It exists because the knowledge base's EU coverage was **EPSO-only**; Ireland,
the largest English-language gap, appeared in no document. It goes deeper than
`eu-frameworks-foundation` rather than restating it.
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
Covered, cited to primary sources opened during research on 2026-07-20:

- PAS / publicjobs as centralised recruiter and the structure of Civil Service
  competitions.
- The **Civil Service Capability Framework (2024)** — its four Capabilities and
  seven dimensions, with grade-by-grade sub-dimensions.
- The **legacy Civil Service Competency Model** — its per-grade competencies and
  the two constants across all grades.
- How PAS interviews run — competency-based vs capability-based, video formats,
  and PAS's published example questions.
- Sector bodies that publish their own selection material: **An Garda Síochána**,
  the **HSE**, and **local authorities**.
- Governance: the **CPSA** Code of Practice and its statutory basis.
- A labelled comparison with UK Civil Service Behaviours and EPSO competencies.

Not covered (deliberately): the Defence Forces (no primary competency framework
was located in this pass — see Open Questions); non-competency selection (e.g.
senior public-service appointments via the Top Level Appointments Committee);
detailed STAR technique (see `techniques-foundation`); and the EU-institution
route, which is EPSO's (see `eu-frameworks-foundation`). Ireland's national
public service is **not** recruited through EPSO; this is a domestic system.
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
Prefer the shared `GLOSSARY.md`; the following are used in the narrowed Irish
sense here.

- **PAS / publicjobs** — the Public Appointments Service, the independent,
  centralised recruiter for the Irish Civil Service and public service; its
  candidate portal and trading name is publicjobs.ie [1] [8].
- **Capability Framework** — the Civil Service framework launched February 2024
  that assesses seven capability dimensions (behaviours, skills, strengths,
  knowledge, values, motivation, interests) under four Capabilities [1] [4] [16].
- **Competency Model** — the legacy Civil Service framework of per-grade
  competencies with "effective performance indicators", still used for some
  roles [5] [6] [7].
- **Capability-Based Interview** — a structured interview that builds a profile
  across capability dimensions and sub-dimensions, exploring the "whole person"
  beyond past behaviour alone [5].
- **CPSA** — the Commission for Public Service Appointments, the statutory
  regulator of public-service recruitment created by the 2004 Act [10] [14].
- **Order of merit / panel** — successful candidates are placed on a panel in
  order of merit, from which vacancies are filled; a panel place is not a
  guarantee of appointment [8].
- **Grades** — the Civil Service general-service grades, ascending: Clerical
  Officer (CO), Executive Officer (EO), Administrative Officer (AO), Higher
  Executive Officer (HEO), Assistant Principal Officer (AP), Principal Officer
  (PO) [1] [6].
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
**Evidence layer — every factual claim cited.**

## Two frameworks, both operative

PAS (publicjobs) committed to "review and update the Civil Service Competency
model to reflect current and anticipated knowledge, skills and behaviour needs,
for all roles from Clerical Officer to Principal Officer"; the resulting
**Civil Service Capability Framework** was developed by Work and Organisational
Psychologists in the PAS Assessment Services Unit and launched in **February
2024** [1] [4] [5] [16]. PAS is explicit that the two systems coexist: "some
roles may utilise the Civil Service Competency Model and others may rely on the
new Capability Framework. Candidates will be informed in advance, about whether
the Competency Model, Capability Framework, or another method/model will be
used" [5]. This document therefore treats **neither** as fully superseded.

## The Capability Framework (2024)

The Capability Framework widens assessment from competencies alone to **seven
capability dimensions**: Behaviours, Skills, Strengths, Knowledge, Values,
Motivation and Interests [1] [3]. These are organised under **four
Capabilities**, each carrying sub-dimensions whose depth increases by grade
[1] [2] [3]:

| Capability | What it covers, verified from the EO framework | Illustrative sub-dimensions across grades |
|-----------|--------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Building Future Readiness** | Embracing change, innovation, digital-first working and continual upskilling | Digital Focus; Openness to Change; Innovation & Creative Solutions; Upskilling for the Future; senior grades add Strategic Awareness & Change / Strategy, Change & Reform |
| **Evidence Informed Delivery** | Evidence-based judgement, prioritisation, resource management and quality outcomes | Delivering Excellence; Managing Work Effectively; Delivering Quality Outcomes & Service; Handling / Analysis, Judgement & Decision Making |
| **Leading and Empowering** | Leading, supporting, developing and empowering people, and leading with specialist insight | Leading, Supporting & Developing; Empowerment, Trust & Honesty; Social & Emotional Awareness; Leading with Specialist Insight |
| **Communicating and Collaborating** | Building relationships and communicating clearly across a whole-of-Government approach | Collaboration, Teamwork & Building Relationships; Effective Communication; Listening, Consulting & Engaging; senior grades split into Communicating & Influencing / Engaging & Collaborating |

Grade shape verified during research: **Clerical Officer** carries only three
Capabilities — Building Future Readiness, Evidence Informed Delivery, and
Communicating and Collaborating — and no "Leading" Capability; the **Executive
Officer** framework adds **Leading and Empowering** [1] [2] [3]. From HEO
upward, "Building Future Readiness" gains a strategy/change sub-dimension and
"Communicating and Collaborating" splits into "Communicating & Influencing" and
"Engaging & Collaborating"; at **Principal Officer** the leadership
sub-dimension is "Leading, Motivating & Inspiring" and delivery adds
"Accountability, Responsibility & Governance" [2]. The framework is published
per grade as an on-screen framework and a downloadable PDF plus an accessible
plaintext version [2] [3].

## The legacy Competency Model

The pre-2024 **Civil Service Competency Model** is a more conventional
competency dictionary: each grade has a fixed set of competencies, each expanded
into "effective performance indicators" [6] [7]. Two competencies are **constant
across every grade** — *Specialist Knowledge, Expertise and Self Development* and
*Drive & Commitment to Public Service Values* — with the remaining competencies
varying by grade [6] [7]. The following are verified from the PAS competency
model documents:

| Grade | Grade-specific competencies, legacy model |
|-------|--------------------------------------------|
| Executive Officer | People Management; Analysis & Decision Making; Delivery of Results; Interpersonal & Communication Skills [6] |
| Higher Executive Officer | Team Leadership; Judgement, Analysis & Decision Making; Management & Delivery of Results; Interpersonal & Communication Skills [7] |

Both grades then add the two constants above [6] [7]. Higher grades shift the
leadership competency upward — for example "Leadership & Strategic Direction" at
Principal Officer — while the delivery, decision-making and communication
competencies persist under grade-appropriate labels [6].

## Comparison with the UK and EPSO — knowledge base analysis

**The mapping below is this knowledge base's own analysis, offered as an
orientation aid. It is not a published concordance; no Irish, UK or EU publisher
states it.** It aligns the four 2024 Irish Capabilities with the UK Civil
Service Behaviours (see `uk-civil-service-success-profiles`) and the EPSO
general competencies (see `epso-competency-framework`).

| Irish Capability (2024) | Nearest UK Civil Service Behaviour(s) | Nearest EPSO competency(ies) |
|-------------------------|----------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Building Future Readiness | Changing and Improving; Seeing the Big Picture | Intrapreneurship; Information management; Learning as a skill |
| Evidence Informed Delivery | Making Effective Decisions; Delivering at Pace; Managing a Quality Service | Decision-making & getting results; Critical thinking, analysing & creative problem-solving |
| Leading and Empowering | Leadership; Developing Self and Others | EPSO has no standalone leadership competency; nearest is Intrapreneurship |
| Communicating and Collaborating | Communicating and Influencing; Working Together | Communication; Working together |

The structural family resemblance is real — all three are behaviour/competency
schemes assessed through structured, evidence-based interviewing — but the
Irish Capability Framework is distinctive in explicitly assessing **strengths,
motivation and interests** alongside behaviours, which the UK scheme handles
through a separate Strengths element and EPSO does not foreground [1] [4].
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
**Evidence layer — every factual claim cited.**

## PAS and how a Civil Service competition is structured

PAS conducts the selection process and "will establish a panel of suitably
qualified individuals to fill vacancies" [8]. A large-volume Civil Service
competition — the 2022 Executive Officer campaign is the worked example — may
comprise, in sequence: computer- and/or paper-based **assessment tests**;
**shortlisting**; an **online video interview**; where relevant **language
tests**; **competitive interview(s)**; and possibly a **presentation/analysis
exercise** or **work-sample test** [8]. Candidates who succeed are placed on a
**panel in order of merit**, from which appointments are made; PAS states
plainly that "qualification and placement on a panel is not a guarantee of
appointment", and candidates may not be reached before the panel expires [8].
The Executive Officer grade is defined as "the entry level to junior management
in the Civil Service", recruited for potential to take on higher responsibility
[8].

## How the interview runs

PAS interviews are structured and take three delivery forms [5]:

- **Automated video interview** — no live panel; questions appear on screen and
  the candidate records responses, which "are later reviewed by our interview
  board members" [5].
- **Live video interview** — a video conference with an interview panel,
  functionally equivalent to a face-to-face interview [5].
- **In-person interview** — a panel of interviewers for some roles [5].

PAS describes its interviews as following "a structured format, with candidates
being questioned and assessed on the competencies or capabilities relevant to
the role. This ensures that all candidates are assessed equally and
transparently" [5].

For **competency-based interviews**, PAS tells candidates the board probes each
example further, and recommends structuring answers with the **STAR** model —
Situation, Task, Action, Result — with PAS's own gloss on each element [5]. For
**capability-based interviews**, PAS explains the aim is "to create a two-way
dialogue that gives insight into the whole person and assesses candidates on
their capability, suitability and readiness for the role", with the board
establishing "a profile and understanding of the candidates' capability across
relevant Capability Dimensions and Sub-Dimensions" [5]. Candidates are told the
capability interview also explores "skills, strengths, knowledge, values,
motivation and interests" — not past behaviour alone [5].

## Sector bodies with their own published selection material

- **An Garda Síochána (national police).** Recruitment of Garda Trainees is run
  in stages: application and selection are "carried out by publicjobs", with
  appointment and training by An Garda Síochána [11]. **Stage 2 is a
  "Competency Based Interview carried out via the Zoom platform"** conducted by
  publicjobs [11]. The official 2025 information booklet documents the process
  and reasonable-accommodation support but **does not list the specific
  competencies assessed** — these are notified to candidates via their
  publicjobs message board before interview [11]. (Third-party preparation
  providers name five — interpersonal skills, service ethos, resilience,
  conscientiousness and problem solving — but this is practitioner material, not
  an official PAS/Garda publication, and is treated here as unverified: see
  Risks & Caveats.)
- **Health Service Executive (HSE).** HSE recruitment is competency-based. Its
  candidate guidance instructs applicants to "describe a situation from your own
  experience" demonstrating each skill, base answers on different situations,
  **not use the same example for more than two competency areas**, and keep each
  answer to a maximum of one A4 page because "the selection board will assess
  your written communication skills on what you write" [12]. At interview, "the
  board may look for more examples of how you demonstrate the skills or
  competencies required" [12]. The guidance stresses that the board will not
  assume competence from job title or length of service alone — evidence of what
  you actually did is required [12].
- **Local authorities.** Local-authority competitions publish a distinct
  competency set. A current Clerical Officer candidate booklet names, for
  example, **Customer Service & Communication Skills, Teamwork, and Information
  Management / Organisational Skills**, and further competencies, with candidates
  required to "demonstrate sufficient evidence within their application form of
  competence under each of these"; shortlisting and interview are based on that
  evidence [13]. The competency labels differ from the Civil Service model,
  confirming that Irish local government maintains its own scheme [13].
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
Questions are grouped by source and by capability. **Provenance:**
*(sourced …)* questions are published by the named source and carry a [n]
citation; *(composed)* questions were written for this knowledge base in the
style of the cited framework and are **not** real or leaked interview questions.

**Gold note:** unusually for this knowledge base, PAS **publishes its own
example questions** for both interview types on its Interview Advice page [5];
those appear first below and are the strongest preparation material available.

## PAS published examples — capability-based interview

1. Please provide a specific example that best demonstrates your capability in the area of Strategy, Change and Reform. *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice, capability-based examples)* [5]
2. Give your best example of utilising technology or digital solutions to address challenges. *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice)* [5]
3. What experience do you have of developing skills and capability within a team? *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice)* [5]
4. How do you foster innovation and creativity in your team? Can you give me an example of this? *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice)* [5]
5. What key skills do you hold to assist you in managing change or reform? *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice)* [5]
6. What strengths have you developed in your career that would help you communicate and influence others? *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice)* [5]
7. What are your most important professional networks and how do they build or support your specialist insight? *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice)* [5]
8. What are your biggest knowledge gaps in relation to digital focus? *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice)* [5]
9. What values underpinned your approach? *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice)* [5]
10. What motivates you to deliver excellence? *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice)* [5]
11. Why is an evidence-informed approach valuable? *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice)* [5]
12. What aspect of your work is the most interesting? *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice)* [5]

## PAS published examples — competency-based interview

1. Tell me about a time when you demonstrated interpersonal skills. *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice, competency-based examples)* [5]
2. Give an example of when you overcame a challenging situation. *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice)* [5]
3. Describe a time when you resolved conflict. *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice)* [5]
4. Have you ever been in a situation where you had to work under pressure? *(sourced: PAS Interview Advice)* [5]

## Building Future Readiness — practice

1. Tell us about a time you adopted a new technology or digital tool to improve how work was done. What efficiency did it deliver? *(composed in the style of the Building Future Readiness capability, Digital Focus)* [3]
2. Describe an occasion when you tried a new approach and it did not work first time. What did you learn and share? *(composed in the style of Openness to Change)* [3]
3. Give an example of a small innovation or improvement you put forward, no matter how minor. *(composed in the style of Innovation & Creative Solutions)* [3]
4. Tell us about how you have kept your skills current for the demands of your role. *(composed in the style of Upskilling for the Future)* [3]

## Evidence Informed Delivery — practice

1. Describe a time you planned and prioritised a heavy workload to hit competing deadlines. *(composed in the style of Managing Work Effectively)* [3]
2. Give an example of a decision you made from incomplete or conflicting information, and the evidence you relied on. *(composed in the style of Informed Judgement & Decision Making)* [3]
3. Tell us about a time you maintained quality and accuracy on a routine task under time pressure. *(composed in the style of Delivering Quality Outcomes & Service)* [3]
4. Describe how you took ownership of a task that had stalled and saw it through. *(composed in the style of the legacy Delivery of Results competency)* [6]

## Leading and Empowering — practice

1. Tell us about a time you got the best out of a team member who was underperforming. *(composed in the style of the legacy People Management competency)* [6]
2. Describe how you developed or coached a colleague. What changed as a result? *(composed in the style of Leading, Supporting & Developing)* [3]
3. Give an example of when you delegated work and showed trust in a colleague. *(composed in the style of Empowerment, Trust & Honesty)* [3]
4. Tell us about a challenging situation where you managed your own emotions and supported others' wellbeing. *(composed in the style of Social & Emotional Awareness)* [3]

## Communicating and Collaborating — practice

1. Describe a time you adapted your communication style to a difficult audience. *(composed in the style of Effective Communication)* [3]
2. Tell us about a situation where you built a working relationship across teams to get something delivered. *(composed in the style of Collaboration, Teamwork & Building Relationships)* [3]
3. Give an example of when you had to influence or negotiate to reach an outcome. *(composed in the style of the senior-grade Influencing & Negotiation sub-dimension)* [3]
4. Describe how you listened to and acted on feedback or stakeholder concerns. *(composed in the style of Listening, Consulting & Engaging)* [3]

## Frontline and sector-body practice

1. Tell us about a time you dealt with a distressed or difficult member of the public and how you responded. *(composed for An Garda Síochána-style frontline assessment)* [11]
2. Describe a situation that tested your resilience. How did you keep going? *(composed for a policing or frontline competency interview)* [11]
3. Give an example, from your own experience, of when you delivered excellent customer service under pressure. *(composed in the style of the HSE and local-authority customer-service competency)* [12] [13]
4. Describe a time you shared information and worked as part of a team to meet a shared objective. *(composed in the style of the local-authority Teamwork competency)* [13]

## Public-service values and specialist knowledge

1. What draws you to a career in the public service, and how do you demonstrate commitment to it? *(composed in the style of Drive & Commitment to Public Service Values)* [6]
2. Tell us about how you have built and applied specialist expertise in your area, and shared it with others. *(composed in the style of Specialist Knowledge, Expertise and Self Development)* [6] [7]
3. Describe a situation where you upheld honesty, ethics or integrity under pressure. *(composed in the style of Drive & Commitment to Public Service Values)* [7]
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
**Coaching layer — guidance, not documented fact. It points back to the cited
evidence and adds no new facts.**

- **First, find out which framework applies.** PAS notifies candidates in
  advance whether a competition uses the Competency Model, the Capability
  Framework, or another method [5]. Confirm this before you prepare — the two
  demand different preparation, as below.
- **For a competency-based interview, use STAR — PAS says so.** PAS explicitly
  recommends Situation, Task, Action, Result and gives its own prompts:
  *when and where*, *what you were expected to do*, *who else was involved*,
  *what you did*, then *how it turned out and how you measured it* [5]. Keep the
  Situation and Task brief and spend most words on your own Action and the
  Result — this mirrors PAS's own emphasis on "what YOU did" [5].
- **For a capability-based interview, prepare more than stories.** PAS says the
  board explores the "whole person" — skills, strengths, knowledge, values,
  motivation and interests [5]. For each relevant Capability, prepare not only a
  behavioural example but also: which key skills you hold, what value your
  specialist knowledge adds, what motivates you, and where your development gaps
  are [5]. PAS's published questions ask about knowledge gaps and motivation
  directly — see the Question Bank — so rehearse honest, self-aware answers, not
  just success stories.
- **Map examples to the sub-dimensions of your grade.** Download the Capability
  Framework for the exact grade [2] [3] and, for each sub-dimension, have at
  least one example ready. Senior grades (HEO and above) will probe strategy,
  reform and influencing — junior grades will not [2] [3].
- **Worked answer skeleton (competency-based, "worked under pressure", a
  Question Bank item).**
  - *Situation/Task (brief):* the deadline or pressure and your specific
    responsibility.
  - *Action (the bulk):* how you prioritised, what you personally did, how you
    kept quality up, who you kept informed — evidence-led, first person.
  - *Result:* the measurable outcome, and what you learned — PAS asks "how did
    you know it went well? how did you measure this?" [5], so quantify.
- **For HSE and local-authority written stages, respect the format rules.** HSE
  caps each competency answer at one A4 page and warns against reusing an
  example for more than two competencies [12]; the written form itself is
  assessed for communication [12]. Plan your examples across competencies so you
  do not double-spend a strong story.
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
**Evidence layer — every factual claim cited.**

- **Structured and equalised by design.** PAS states its interviews follow a
  structured format so "all candidates are assessed equally and transparently",
  questioning candidates on the competencies or capabilities relevant to the
  role [5]. In automated video interviews the recorded responses are reviewed
  afterwards by "interview board members" [5].
- **Evidence over inference.** HSE assessor-facing guidance is explicit that the
  review team "will not assume you demonstrate a skill only because of your
  current role, length of experience or educational qualifications" and relies
  on the detail the candidate provides about "what you actually did and how you
  did it" [12]. This is a documented articulation of the evidence principle that
  underlies competency scoring.
- **Written communication is itself scored.** For competency application forms,
  HSE tells candidates "the selection board will assess your written
  communication skills on what you write on your application form", and imposes a
  one-A4-page limit the board "will take into account" [12].
- **Order of merit and panels.** PAS places successful candidates on a panel in
  order of merit and fills vacancies from it; a place is not an offer, and the
  panel may expire before a candidate is reached [8]. This is the Irish
  equivalent of a reserve list.
- **Governed selection.** Under the CPSA Code of Practice, a public body must run
  a fair, transparent and merit-based selection process; appointment on merit
  means "the appointment of the best person to any given post … through a
  transparent, competitive recruitment process", with selection criteria
  "related directly to the qualifications, personal attributes and skills
  required" of the post [9]. The Code sets **six recruitment principles**:
  probity, merit, best practice, consistency, transparency, and appointments
  promoting equality, diversity and inclusion [9]. Review and complaint
  procedures are mandatory [9] [10].
- **What is not published.** No PAS document located in this pass publishes a
  numerical marking scale or per-competency scoring descriptors for interviews,
  unlike, say, the UK Success Profiles 7-point scale. PAS provides departments
  with "draft interview and scoring templates" as internal client tools [16], but
  these are not public. Treat any specific Irish interview scoring rubric in
  circulation as unverified.
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
**Coaching layer — analysis, clearly labelled. Not cited fact.**

- **Assuming the framework.** The commonest error is preparing for a
  capability-based interview when the role uses the legacy Competency Model, or
  vice versa. PAS tells you which applies in advance [5] — read the message
  board and information booklet.
- **Treating a capability interview like a competency interview.** Bringing only
  STAR stories to a capability-based interview under-answers it: PAS also probes
  strengths, knowledge gaps, values, motivation and interests [5]. Candidates
  who cannot articulate a development gap or what motivates them lose ground.
- **Reusing one strong story everywhere.** HSE explicitly limits an example to
  at most two competency areas [12]; recycling a single achievement signals thin
  evidence to any Irish public-service board.
- **"We" instead of "I".** PAS's own prompts foreground "what YOU did" and how
  you personally measured success [5]; boards cannot credit a contribution you
  do not claim clearly.
- **Ignoring the one-page limit or format rules.** On HSE and local-authority
  forms, overrunning the page limit or writing vaguely costs marks because the
  written form is itself assessed [12] [13].
- **Confusing composed practice questions with real ones.** The *(composed)*
  questions above are stylistic imitations built from the cited descriptors —
  presenting them, or any coach's list, as "real PAS questions" misleads.
  PAS's genuinely published examples are only those labelled *(sourced)* [5].
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
**Coaching layer.**

1. **Framework check (10 min).** Before anything else, confirm from your
   competition's information booklet and publicjobs message board whether the
   role uses the Capability Framework or the Competency Model [5]. Write it at
   the top of your prep notes.
2. **Sub-dimension mapping (60–90 min).** Download the Capability Framework PDF
   for your exact grade [2] [3]. For each sub-dimension, draft one STAR example
   and note the skills, strengths and knowledge you would cite; flag any
   sub-dimension where you have no example — that is your gap to close.
3. **PAS-question drill (45 min).** Take the twelve published capability
   questions above [5]. Answer each aloud in under two minutes, then self-check:
   did you give a specific example *and* address the skill/strength/value the
   question targets?
4. **Whole-person rehearsal (30 min).** Practise the three PAS questions that are
   not behavioural — knowledge gaps, what motivates you, why evidence matters
   [5]. These trip up candidates who only rehearse success stories.
5. **Written-form discipline (for HSE / local authority).** Write one competency
   answer to the exact one-A4-page limit; check you have not reused the example
   elsewhere and that a stranger could tell exactly what *you* did [12] [13].
6. **Mock board (half day, for panels).** Using the four Capabilities and their
   grade sub-dimensions [3], build a five-question structured interview with an
   agreed evidence checklist per question; run it, and compare notes before
   discussing to reduce single-assessor drift.
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
**Evidence layer — every factual claim cited. Signposts only; not legal advice.**

- **Statutory basis of selection.** The **Public Service Management (Recruitment
  and Appointments) Act 2004** established the Public Appointments Service and
  the Commission for Public Service Appointments, and underpins the licensing and
  regulation of public-service recruitment in Ireland [14].
- **Regulator's Code of Practice.** The CPSA Code of Practice requires
  appointments to be made fairly, transparently and on the basis of merit, built
  on six recruitment principles, with mandatory review and complaint procedures
  for candidates dissatisfied with a process [9] [10]. Selection criteria must
  relate directly to the requirements of the post [9].
- **Equality law.** Irish recruitment is subject to the **Employment Equality
  Act 1998** (as amended), which prohibits discrimination in employment —
  including in the arrangements for deciding who should be offered employment —
  across nine grounds: gender, civil status, family status, sexual orientation,
  religion, age, disability, race, and membership of the Traveller community
  [15]. Interview design, questioning and scoring fall within its scope.
- **Reasonable accommodations.** PAS states it is "committed to positively
  supporting candidates with disabilities throughout the selection process" and
  invites candidates to request accommodations via the recruitment unit or its
  Disability Champion [5]; the Garda Trainee process gives the same undertaking
  [11]. The duty to make reasonable accommodation for disabled applicants is a
  requirement of the Employment Equality Acts [15].
- **Candidate data.** As an EU member state, Ireland processes candidate
  personal data under the GDPR; PAS manages applications and communications
  through the publicjobs message-board account [5] [8]. For the EU data regime
  see `best-practice-foundation`.
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
- **Two live frameworks — high confusion risk.** Because the Competency Model and
  the Capability Framework coexist and are chosen per role [5], any general
  advice — including this document — can mislead if the reader does not confirm
  which governs their competition.
- **Transition is recent and ongoing.** The Capability Framework was launched
  February 2024 and, per gov.ie, was being rolled out across large-volume
  campaigns with a Principal Officer pilot [4] [5] [16]; grade-level detail and
  templates may still evolve. Verified as published on 2026-07-20.
- **Garda competencies are a verified negative in the public record.** The
  official 2025 booklet documents a publicjobs-run competency interview and
  role-play but does **not** publish the specific competencies [11]; the widely
  cited "five competencies" come only from third-party training providers and
  are treated here as unverified per the source-priority rule — practitioner
  material is never the sole support for a process claim.
- **No public interview marking scale.** Unlike the UK Success Profiles 7-point
  scale, no PAS numerical interview scoring rubric or per-competency descriptor
  set was located publicly; scoring templates exist but are internal client
  tools [4]. Do not import a UK or EPSO scale and imply it is Irish.
- **Sector coverage is uneven.** HSE and local-authority coverage rests on a
  candidate-guidance page and one representative local-authority booklet
  [12] [13]; practice varies by employer and competition. The Defence Forces
  were not covered for want of a located primary framework.
- **The UK/EPSO comparison is inference.** The mapping table is the knowledge
  base's analysis, not a concordance any body publishes; it caps the document at
  Medium confidence.
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

text:
- Does the Defence Forces (Óglaigh na hÉireann) publish a competency framework or
  competency-based interview specification for officer or enlisted entry? Not
  located in this pass; a military.ie / Defence Forces careers review would
  resolve it.
- Will PAS publish per-grade capability marking descriptors or a public scoring
  scale, or retire the legacy Competency Model entirely? Watching publicjobs.ie
  and gov.ie renewal publications would resolve this.
- What are the current, officially named competencies for the Garda Trainee
  competency interview, and does An Garda Síochána intend to publish them rather
  than issue them privately to candidates [11]?
- How consistent is the local-authority competency set across the 31 local
  authorities, and is it centrally defined, for example by the Local Government
  Management Agency? Only one authority's booklet was examined here [13].
- Do An Garda Síochána, the HSE and local authorities intend to adopt the Civil
  Service Capability Framework, or will they retain their own schemes?
title: Ireland: Public Appointments Service Competency Frameworks

title: Assessment Centre Exercises: Group Tasks, In-Trays & Presentations

text:
An assessment centre is not one long interview. It is a multi-exercise,
multi-assessor event in which the *same* competencies are sampled several
different ways, and the competency interview is only one of the scored
exercises. This document is the deep-dive on everything *around* that
interview: group and team exercises, in-tray and e-tray simulations, written
exercises and case studies, prepared and unseen presentations, role-plays with
trained actors, and situational judgement tests where they form part of the
selection day. For each it records what the exercise is, which competencies it
is documented as targeting, how it is observed and scored, and how long it
runs.

The strongest published evidence in this space is UK public-sector. The
College of Policing publishes, exercise by exercise, exactly which Competency
and Values Framework (CVF) competencies each of its national exercises
assesses — a written exercise scored on *respect and empathy*, *we analyse
critically*, *we support and inspire* and *we collaborate*, and a twelve-question
briefing exercise scored on *public service*, *we are emotionally aware*, *we
take ownership* and *we are innovative and open-minded* — together with the
timings, the preparation rules and the automatic-fail policy [2]. Its
values-based recruitment guidance publishes the assessor-side machinery in
equal detail: the **ORCE** model (observe, record, classify, evaluate), an A–D
competency rating scale with descriptors, a catalogue of assessor biases with
countermeasures, and the finding that arithmetic (score-based) decision making
outperforms group discussion [4]. Cabinet Office SCS guidance publishes an
assessment matrix mapping each Success Profiles element to each exercise, and
instructs panels to "observe, record, classify, evaluate and score each
question independently" before agreeing an overall score [5]. Private-sector
practice is documented far more thinly: employers name their stages — Deloitte's
job simulation and final-stage topic discussion [18], KPMG's half-day Launch
Pad [19], Unilever's Discovery Centre [20], Barclays' multi-stage centre [21] —
but rarely publish exercise briefs or scoring criteria.

Confidence is **Medium**. The exercise-by-exercise competency mappings,
timings, rating scales and assessor process rest on official College of
Policing, Cabinet Office, GOV.UK and EPSO documentation. But the generic
taxonomy of exercise types — assigned versus unassigned group roles,
in-tray prioritisation heuristics, planned versus on-the-spot presentations —
rests substantially on named university careers services rather than on
employer or regulator documents, and several named employers publish stage
names without exercise detail.
title: Assessment Centre Exercises: Group Tasks, In-Trays & Presentations

text:
- The Civil Service documents six named job-related simulations through which
  behaviours may be assessed: presentation, in-tray exercise, written
  analysis/exercise, group exercise/discussion, role-play and oral briefing
  [1]. Every exercise in this document maps to that list. *(documented)*
- The College of Policing publishes a **per-exercise competency map**: its
  written exercise assesses four named CVF competencies and values, its
  briefing exercise assesses a different four, and its competency-based
  interview a third set of five [2]. Exercises are deliberately not
  interchangeable — each samples a different slice of the framework.
  *(documented)*
- Assessment centres are defined by professional-body guidance as a
  **multi-rater, multi-method** approach — multiple exercises, multiple
  assessors, several dimensions — whose validity depends on candidates
  receiving "the same types and numbers of tasks to complete within the same
  timeframe" [4][12]. *(documented)*
- The published assessor discipline is **ORCE**: observe, record (verbatim,
  no interpretation), classify into competencies only after the candidate has
  left, then evaluate — each stage carried out independently of other assessors
  before any discussion [4]. Cabinet Office SCS guidance uses the same
  observe/record/classify/evaluate sequence for interview panels [5].
  *(documented)*
- **Arithmetic decision making beats discussion.** College of Policing guidance
  states that ranking candidates by score "has been shown to be the most valid"
  decision method, while assessors discussing performances and deciding
  together "have been shown to be less effective... and are difficult to
  justify from a fairness point of view" [4]. *(documented)*
- In-tray and e-tray exercises are the *same* exercise in different media, and
  are scored on prioritisation and justification rather than on reaching a
  single correct answer: "there is no one right answer, the important thing is
  to provide logical and clearly explain the reasons for your decisions" [14].
  *(documented)*
- Group exercises come in two documented shapes: **unassigned/leaderless**,
  where candidates get "individual briefing documents, with different
  information to each other" and must reach a joint decision, and **assigned
  role**, where a candidate is asked to chair or lead [15]. The competency being
  sampled is different in each. *(documented)*
- For candidates: contribution quality, not airtime, is what is recorded. The
  observation unit is a behaviour classified against a named competency [4], so
  three well-evidenced interventions score better than continuous talking that
  classifies into nothing. *(guidance)*
- For designers: the CVF indicators alone are "not sufficient" for a complex
  exercise — you must write exercise-specific assessor guidance derived from a
  pilot with good performers, and it must be guidance, "not a 'model answer'"
  [4]. *(documented)*
title: Assessment Centre Exercises: Group Tasks, In-Trays & Presentations

text:
This document answers two questions.

For **candidates**: "the invitation says assessment centre — what are the
exercises that are not the interview, what competency is each one actually
measuring, and how do I prepare for each without over-rehearsing?" It gives
published exercise briefs, timings, the observable behaviours assessors are
told to record, and drills.

For **interviewers, HR and assessment designers**: "if I am building or running
a selection day, what does documented UK/EU practice say about exercise choice,
competency-to-exercise mapping, assessor training, rating scales, scoring
mechanics and the biases the design has to defend against?" The Assessor
Perspective section is written to be usable as a design checklist, because the
College of Policing and Cabinet Office publish enough of their own machinery to
support one.
title: Assessment Centre Exercises: Group Tasks, In-Trays & Presentations

text:
**Covered:** the non-interview exercises that surround a competency interview at
an assessment centre, in UK and EU public and private practice — group and team
exercises (assigned and unassigned roles); in-tray and e-tray exercises;
written exercises and case studies; presentations, both prepared in advance and
set on the day; role-plays and simulations with role actors; and situational
judgement tests where they form part of the selection day. For each: what it
is, which competencies it targets, how it is observed and scored, and how long
it runs. Also covered: the assessor-side mechanics that make an exercise
scoreable — ORCE, rating scales, exercise-specific assessor guidance, assessor
bias, and score-based versus discussion-based decisions.

**Not covered:** the competency interview itself as a format (see
`formats-foundation` and `panel-interviews`); asynchronous video mechanics (see
`video-asynchronous-interviews`); STAR and other answer techniques (see
`techniques-foundation`); and the **EPSO assessment-centre day end-to-end**,
which is the subject of `eu-institutions-assessment-centres`. EPSO material
appears here only as a comparator for exercise *design*, cross-referenced
rather than repeated. Ability and psychometric testing is covered only where it
runs inside the assessment day. Technical and academic-panel exercises are out
of scope except where they carry competency scoring.
title: Assessment Centre Exercises: Group Tasks, In-Trays & Presentations

text:
Narrow, document-specific usages; see the shared glossary for general terms.

- **Exercise** — one scored, time-bounded activity within an assessment centre,
  producing behavioural evidence against one or more named competencies.
- **Work sample test** — an exercise that "allows the assessors to see the
  candidate in action in an activity that represents a sample of the role",
  as distinct from an interview where the candidate talks about past or
  hypothetical action [4].
- **Unassigned-role (leaderless) group exercise** — a group task in which no
  candidate is designated leader and candidates typically hold different
  briefing information from one another [15].
- **Assigned-role group exercise** — a group task in which a candidate is
  explicitly asked to chair a meeting or lead the group [15].
- **In-tray / e-tray exercise** — a simulation of the administrative flow of a
  role: a set of emails, memos, reports, messages and documents to read,
  prioritise and act on within a time limit. The two terms describe the same
  exercise, paper-based versus computer-based [14][16].
- **Briefing exercise** — an exercise in which the candidate takes a role, is
  given preparation material, and responds verbally to a structured set of
  questions about the situation; the College of Policing runs a twelve-question,
  three-part version nationally [2].
- **Role actor** — a trained person who plays a character (customer, colleague,
  member of the public) in a role-play, observed by a separate assessor [4][15].
- **ORCE** — observe, record, classify, evaluate: the linear assessor process
  used to convert observed behaviour into a competency rating [4].
- **Exercise-specific assessor guidance** — a document translating generic
  competency indicators into observable, exercise-specific evidence statements,
  produced by piloting the exercise with existing good performers [4].
- **Adverse impact** — a selection method producing a minority-group pass rate
  materially below the majority rate; commonly evaluated using the 80 per cent
  (four-fifths) rule, which "does not have a legal footing" in the UK [4].
title: Assessment Centre Exercises: Group Tasks, In-Trays & Presentations

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

## Where exercises sit in UK Civil Service Success Profiles

Success Profiles is explicitly exercise-plural. GOV.UK guidance on Civil
Service Behaviours lists the ways behaviours may be assessed as including an
application form requiring written examples, a Civil Service judgement test, a
personality test, an interview and an assessment centre — and names six
job-related simulations: a **presentation**, an **in-tray exercise**, a
**written analysis/exercise**, a **group exercise/discussion**, a **role-play**
and an **oral briefing** [1]. Behaviours may also be assessed through
situational judgement tests or situational interview questions asking how a
candidate would behave in hypothetical scenarios [1]. Civil Service Careers
tells candidates that an assessment centre "will usually consist of a
combination of different selection methods, such as an interview, a
presentation, a written exercise and/or a role-play exercise" [8].

At Senior Civil Service level the exercise mix is published as an explicit
matrix. Cabinet Office guidance for HR Director recruitment sets out a
sifting stage, a pre-interview leadership assessment comprising an **Individual
Leadership Assessment (ILA)** and a **Staff Engagement Exercise (SEE)** — both
of which "are not scored", with findings and suggested probing questions shared
with the interview panel — and an interview assessment which "will also
includes a 5-minute presentation covering a set topic related to the
candidate's leadership experience" [5]. The matrix maps which Success Profiles
element each exercise carries: technical and behaviours at sift; behaviours at
the staff engagement exercise; technical, behaviours, strengths and experience
at interview and presentation [5].

## Where exercises sit in the police Competency and Values Framework

The College of Policing runs a national, multi-exercise assessment process for
police constable recruitment and publishes the competency mapping for each
exercise. For initial police recruitment "the competencies review criteria is
set at level 1" [2][3]. The online assessment process consists of three
exercises — a competency-based interview, a **written exercise** and a
**briefing exercise** [2] — preceded by a sift which is either in-force or the
**national sift**, itself made up of a situational judgement test and a
behavioural styles questionnaire [3].

| Exercise | CVF competencies and values assessed | Published timing |
|----------|--------------------------------------|------------------|
| Competency-based interview | courage; public service; respect and empathy; we take ownership; we are innovative and open-minded [2] | ~40 minutes; five questions; 60 s thinking + 300 s answer each [2] |
| Written exercise | respect and empathy; we analyse critically; we support and inspire; we collaborate [2] | ~10 min reading + ~40 min work, hard cap 120 minutes [2] |
| Briefing exercise | public service; we are emotionally aware; we take ownership; we are innovative and open-minded [2] | ~60 minutes total: ~10 min untimed preparation, then ~50 min of 12 questions in three parts, 60 s thinking + 180 s answer each [2] |
| Situational judgement test (national sift) | we analyse critically; we are innovative and open-minded; we take ownership; we are emotionally aware; we collaborate; we support and inspire [3] | untimed, expected ~45 minutes [3] |
| Behavioural styles questionnaire (national sift) | the six SJT competencies plus respect and empathy; courage; public service [3] | untimed, expected ~20 minutes [3] |

Note that these names are the **2024 CVF** names ("we collaborate", "we support
and inspire"), not the superseded 2016 wording — see Risks & Caveats.

## Where exercises sit in EU-institution selection

EPSO's Assessment Centre assesses general and/or specific competencies "by
means of different exercises, the content of which is validated by the
selection board: a case study, an oral presentation, a structured interview, an
interview in the field, an e-tray, etc.", with candidates "observed/evaluated
in a systematic way by at least 2 trained assessors" in each exercise [6].
EPSO publishes complete candidate assignment booklets for some exercises; the
booklet for the situational competency-based interview in open competitions
EPSO/AD/365-370/19 states that the exercise "is designed to assess the
following competencies: Analysis & Problem Solving, Learning & Development,
Prioritising & Organising, Resilience, Working with Others, and Leadership",
that "no previous knowledge is required", and that "the content of this
document is fictitious" though "derived from actual events" [7]. The
competency labels in that 2020 booklet predate EPSO's 2023 competency
framework — the EU-side treatment belongs to `eu-institutions-assessment-centres`
and is not restated here.

## Where exercises sit in UK graduate schemes

Named employers publish stage structures rather than exercise briefs.
**Deloitte** publishes a four-stage assessed process whose third stage is a
**Job Simulation** using work-based scenarios in which candidates "rank options
in order of preference" and give "written responses and record video
responses", assessing behavioural skills, and whose final stage combines a
~30-minute **topic discussion** ("a two-way discussion") with a ~50-minute
skills and motivation interview; the topic discussion requires preparation
"researching and conducting analysis on a topic" [18]. **KPMG** publishes a
half-day, in-person **Launch Pad** that "combines assessed and non-assessed
activities" at which candidates demonstrate the "skills needed to succeed in
your role", with outcomes issued within two working days [19]. **Unilever**
publishes a **Discovery Centre** described as "a 'day in the life' of a Future
Leader at Unilever... filled with several exciting activities including project
meetings, business challenges, a team meeting with Unilever colleagues, and an
interview" [20]. **Barclays** documents an assessment centre of "two or three
stages" including "a motivational interview with a member of the leadership
team", at which candidates demonstrate "the relevant strengths, behaviours or
technical skills required for the role" [21]. The **NHS Graduate Management
Training Scheme** runs online tests followed by interviews and an assessment
centre assessing "communication, numeracy, planning and analytical thinking
skills" [22].

Careers-service reporting places the typical centre at "a half or full day",
combining presentations, team exercises, written tests, interviews and
psychometric tests, and lets employers "compare the performance of lots of
candidates at the same time" [17].
title: Assessment Centre Exercises: Group Tasks, In-Trays & Presentations

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

## The assessment centre as a method

Assessment centres are "a multi-rater, multi-method approach... candidates are
given multiple exercises or opportunities to demonstrate their suitability for
the role and are assessed by multiple people in the course of that assessment
across several dimensions — usually competencies" [4]. In practice they "often
look like a combination of several different work-sample type exercises, eg, a
presentation, a written report and a roleplay exercise" [4]. The documented
trade-off is explicit: "well-designed assessment centres can have very good
validity but they are time consuming and costly and need to be carefully
designed to ensure they are accurate and valid predictors of performance"; the
major benefit is "a very thorough and detailed assessment of each candidate in
multiple scenarios and by different people, thereby being more likely to see
the candidate's real or typical performance than a singular one-off interview
or test" [4]. CIPD describes them as requiring candidates "to complete several
different tasks", often combining "behavioural ratings, cognitive and
personality assessments obtained from multiple sources", and requires that
tasks be "administered in a systematic way, with candidates being given the
same types and numbers of tasks to complete within the same timeframe, so that
they have equal opportunity to demonstrate their abilities" [12].

The underlying logic of the non-interview exercises is the **work sample**:
"while interviews allow a candidate to talk about things they have done or
things they may do in the future, a work sample test allows the assessors to
see the candidate in action in an activity that represents a sample of the
role" — an analysis task for an analytical role, a community engagement
exercise for a police officer [4]. Work sample exercises "often assess task
performance or skill as well as the demonstration of competencies or values (eg,
a teaching task may assess empathy and communication skills, as well as
accurately describing the subject matter)" [4]. CIPD reports that skill-based
assessments "have been found to be better predictors of performance on the job
when compared with traditional approaches such as tests, reviewing job
experience, education, or unstructured interviews" [12].

## Group and team exercises

Two documented structures. In an **unassigned-role (leaderless)** group
exercise, candidates receive "individual briefing documents, with different
information to each other" and must "make a group decision, whilst using
negotiation and compromising skills" [15]. In an **assigned-role** exercise,
organisations test leadership by asking a candidate to "chair a meeting or lead
a group", with assessors observing delegation, leveraging team strengths,
monitoring progress, maintaining focus and ensuring decisions are reached [15].
Other documented group formats include ice-breakers intended to help candidates
relax, practical problem-solving tasks with physical items, and discussions on
industry-relevant topics [15]. University of York guidance describes the same
family — discussions on employer-relevant topics, current affairs or news
items, which "may involve case studies or assigned leadership roles", and group
tasks such as "a practical activity (eg building a tower out of straws) or
problem solving task based on a workplace scenario; it could involve role play"
[16].

The observation model is that "assessors observe a small group of candidates
and may 'score' candidates against a list of skills or attributes they are
looking for", attending to teamwork, collaboration, communication,
problem-solving, originality of ideas and the ability to work cooperatively
"without dominating" [16].

Group exercises are the natural home of the Civil Service "group
exercise/discussion" simulation [1] and of collaboration-family competencies —
the College of Policing scores *we collaborate* in its written exercise and
across its national sift [2][3].

## In-tray and e-tray exercises

E-tray and in-tray exercises "are essentially the same thing, with the only
difference being that one is computer-based while the other is paper-based",
and are "usually given to an individual at an assessment centre, however they
can sometimes be given as a group exercise" [14]. Candidates receive
"background information on the company, your role within it and a scenario"
along with materials such as "memos, emails, telephone messages, reports and
letters" [14]. University of York guidance describes the same design: the
exercise simulates "the administrative aspects of the job. You are given a
range of material which could include emails, letters, notes and phone
messages", and "you must decide on your priorities for responding to these and
deal appropriately with each item. You may need to draft replies and will need
to justify your reasoning" [16]. Kent's guidance adds that candidates are given
their role, an organisational chart and objectives, and must "prioritise the
items in order" — analysing reports, drafting replies, delegating tasks —
within a time limit [15].

**What is assessed.** Kent lists the competencies as "prioritisation, analysis,
working under pressure, problem solving, adaptability, organising, decision
making and communication" [15]. LSE lists the assessed abilities as absorbing
information "quickly and accurately", prioritising "this information and any
arising actions", and planning and making decisions "within a time constraint",
alongside time management, analytical thinking, written communication and
commercial awareness [14]. York adds reading comprehension and performance
under pressure [16].

**How it is scored.** LSE's guidance is explicit that "there is no one right
answer, the important thing is to provide logical and clearly explain the
reasons for your decisions" [14]. The published prioritisation cues are the
sender of the item, when it was written, whether deadlines are mentioned,
confidentiality markings, dependencies on other tasks, and business impact;
the recommended sequence is most urgent tasks, then urgent non-trivial tasks,
then trivial tasks [14].

**How long.** LSE's worked example is a 90-minute task in which candidates
should "spend no more than 15 minutes" on the initial read of the materials
[14].

## Written exercises and case studies

The College of Policing publishes a fully specified national written exercise:
the candidate takes "the role of a police constable and complete[s] an urgent
written task for your line manager", is "provided with four items of
information", and is instructed to "only use the information provided and do
not add or invent any information"; all answers go "in the same response box"
[2]. The instructions take approximately 10 minutes to read, the exercise
"should take approximately 40 minutes to complete", and a hard limit of 120
minutes applies, with whatever is in the text box at time-out being submitted
[2]. Two design decisions are published and unusually candid: **spelling and
grammar are not assessed** ("we appreciate candidates will be completing the
written exercise on different devices and some may have access to spell-check
while others may not... to ensure a fair approach, we will not be assessing
candidates' spelling or grammar"), and **pasting text in from another
application "will not be accepted and may result in an automatic failure"** [2].

Case studies are documented as individual or group exercises requiring analysis
of real-world problems with "qualitative and quantitative data", in which
candidates construct arguments supporting their conclusions, "sometimes
presented or submitted as written recommendations", assessing analysis,
problem-solving, communication, commercial awareness, working under pressure
and decision making [15]. York describes the case study as "based on a
fictional situation, simulating the kind of business problem you are likely to
come across in the role you have applied for. You are given a set of
documents... and a problem to solve", with output as "a written report and/or a
presentation, and can expect questions on your recommendations"; assessors
attend to analytical thinking, logical reasoning, commercial awareness and the
ability to justify recommendations persuasively [16]. EPSO lists a case study
as one of its Assessment Centre exercises [6].

## Presentations — prepared and unseen

Two documented modes. **Planned** presentations give advance instructions and
research time and typically run 5–10 minutes on industry-related topics;
**on-the-spot** presentations assign the topic at the assessment centre and
evaluate "performance under pressure and verbal communication skills" [15].
York records that candidates "may prepare talks in advance or be assigned
topics at the assessment centre", with assessors attending to communication,
time management, delivery under pressure, body language and the ability to
respond to questions [16].

The Civil Service publishes a concrete prepared-presentation specification at
Director level: a **5-minute presentation covering a set topic related to the
candidate's leadership experience**, delivered within a blended interview that
assesses experience, technical skills, behaviours and strengths [5]. Deloitte's
final stage includes a ~30-minute **topic discussion** — "a two-way discussion"
with an assessor asking questions — for which candidates must prepare by
"researching and conducting analysis on a topic" [18]. EPSO runs an oral
presentation as a named Assessment Centre exercise [6].

## Briefing exercises and oral briefings

The Civil Service names "oral briefing" as a simulation type [1]. The College
of Policing publishes a national example in full. The candidate takes the role
of a police constable "and will have responsibility for dealing with some
issues presented to you", provides verbal responses to questions about those
issues, and is given preparation materials, using only the information provided
[2]. The structure: approximately 10 minutes of untimed, unassessed preparation
on part one, then approximately 50 minutes across three parts of four questions
each — 12 questions in total — with new information introduced at parts two and
three, and for every question 60 seconds of thinking time and 180 seconds to
answer [2]. Each question is presented both on screen and read aloud by a
pre-recorded assessor video [2]. Candidates are told to "consider the issues
raised and how the police can build positive relationships" [2].

## Role-plays and simulations

Role-plays give the candidate "a brief" outlining the situation and their
assigned role and a "limited amount of time to prepare"; an actor plays a
character such as a customer or colleague while a separate assessor observes,
with communication and negotiation skills the primary focus, particularly for
customer-facing roles [15]. The College of Policing's worked design example is
a role-play on a diversity topic, and it shows the exercise-specific assessor
guidance side by side with the generic CVF indicators — for *we are emotionally
aware* (level 2), the observable evidence statements include "candidate
specifically asks the role actor to challenge their thinking", "candidate
addresses the role actor's inappropriate language about Mr Walker" and
"candidate communicates sensitively and warmly but changes style to challenge
role actor's inappropriate behaviour immediately and firmly when required" [4].

Simulations may also be delivered remotely. Deloitte's Job Simulation presents
work-based scenarios in which candidates rank options by preference and submit
written and recorded video responses [18].

## Situational judgement tests within the selection day

The College of Policing publishes its national SJT design in full. The test
"assesses your judgement and decision-making skills in job-relevant
situations"; each scenario presents four possible actions, and candidates rate
each action on a five-point effectiveness scale whose anchors are published
verbatim: **counterproductive** ("an inappropriate action that will have a
negative impact or make the situation worse"), **ineffective** ("a poor action
which will not help to resolve the situation"), **slightly effective**,
**effective** ("a reasonable action that will help to resolve the situation")
and **very effective** ("one of the best actions that could be taken to resolve
the situation") [3]. Candidates must rate each action independently, may give
the same rating to more than one action, and "do not have to use the full range
of the rating scale" [3]. The exercise is untimed but expected to take about 45
minutes [3].

Alongside it runs a **behavioural styles questionnaire** measuring "typical
behaviour and preferences at work" on a published seven-point agree/disagree
scale, with forced-choice ranking of statements triggered "depending on your
responses" [3]. Both are automatically scored by the platform; SJT and BSQ
scores "are combined to give an overall score", and overall performance is
"scored against a comparison group of applicants to policing roles displayed as
a percentile" [3].

More generally, SJTs "give candidates a number of scenarios and usually a series
of answers which the candidate has to rank or rate according to their
effectiveness"; they "tend to be best when they are designed specifically for
the role (as opposed to an off-the-shelf test)", are objectively marked, and
"tend to have lower adverse impact than ability tests" — but "typically have
lower score ranges and validity, however, and therefore may only accurately sift
out the lowest performers" [4]. Ability tests, by contrast, "have a tendency to
cause adverse impact whereby white candidates perform significantly better than
candidates from some black and minority ethnic backgrounds" and "should only be
selected, used and interpreted by those who are appropriately trained to do so
through the British Psychological Society qualifications in test use" [4].

## Assessed-throughout and non-assessed elements

Not everything on the day is scored, and centres publish this inconsistently.
KPMG's Launch Pad "combines assessed and non-assessed activities" alongside
networking [19]. The Civil Service's Individual Leadership Assessment and Staff
Engagement Exercise "are not scored", but their findings are shared with the
interview panel together with suggested probing questions [5]. Conversely,
careers-service guidance warns that "you may be assessed throughout the time
you are with the employer — on a company tour or even at lunch or in the bar in
the evening", with assessors evaluating candidates' behaviour toward all staff
[16]. The College of Policing publishes an **automatic fail policy**: conduct
contravening the Code of Ethics, behaving inappropriately toward others, or
acting "to obtain an unfair advantage over other candidates" is referred to an
independent Quality Assurance team, and an automatic fail "means that you fail
the assessment process regardless of your performance in the individual
exercises" and cannot be appealed [2].
title: Assessment Centre Exercises: Group Tasks, In-Trays & Presentations

text:
Realistic exercise briefs and prompts, plus the competency questions asked in
exercise debriefs. Items marked *(sourced …)* are published verbatim or in
close paraphrase by the named source; items marked *(composed)* were written
for this knowledge base in the style of the cited frameworks and are **not**
real, leaked or employer-attributed material.

## Group and team exercises

1. You and four other candidates are a project group. Each of you has been given a different briefing document; between you, you hold all the information needed. In 30 minutes, agree a single recommendation to the board and nominate one person to present it. *(composed — in the style of the unassigned-role group exercise described by University of Kent Careers)* [15]
2. Your group must recommend which three of the eight funding bids attached should be approved. Your own brief argues for bid four. The group must reach a unanimous decision. *(composed — unassigned-role, conflicting-brief design)* [15]
3. You have been asked to chair this meeting. You have 25 minutes to bring the group to a documented decision and allocate follow-up actions. *(composed — in the style of the assigned-role/leadership group exercise described by University of Kent Careers)* [15]
4. Using only the materials on the table, your group has 20 minutes to build a structure that meets the specification in your brief. *(composed — in the style of the practical group task described by University of York Careers)* [16]
5. As a group, discuss the news item in your pack and agree what your organisation's public position should be. *(composed — in the style of the current-affairs group discussion described by University of York Careers)* [16]
6. Your group is one member short because a candidate has withdrawn. Redistribute the roles in your brief and proceed. *(composed — adaptability probe)*
7. Two members of your group disagree on the recommendation and there are four minutes left. What do you do? *(composed — observed decision-forcing prompt)*

## In-tray and e-tray exercises

1. You have returned from two weeks' leave. Your inbox contains 18 items — emails, memos, telephone messages, reports and letters. You have 90 minutes. Prioritise the items, act on them, and justify your reasoning. *(composed — in the style of the 90-minute in-tray design described by LSE Careers)* [14]
2. Read the background information on the organisation, your role within it and the scenario, then work through your tray. Spend no more than 15 minutes on the initial read. *(sourced: LSE Careers, "E-tray and in-tray exercises")* [14]
3. For each item, state whether you would action it now, delegate it, defer it or take no action, and give one sentence of justification. *(composed — the "justify your reasoning" requirement documented by University of York Careers)* [16]
4. Item 7 is marked confidential and contradicts item 3, which came from your director an hour earlier. How do you handle the conflict? *(composed — dependency/confidentiality cue from the published prioritisation criteria)* [14]
5. Draft a reply to the customer complaint at item 12. You have eight minutes. *(composed — drafting sub-task within an in-tray)* [15]
6. New information has just arrived in your tray at the 60-minute mark. Revise your priority order and explain what changed. *(composed — staged-injection design)*

## Written exercises and case studies

1. You are a police constable. Complete an urgent written task for your line manager, using only the four items of information provided; do not add or invent any information. *(sourced: College of Policing, "Online assessment process: Candidate guidance", September 2024 — the national written exercise brief)* [2]
2. You are given a set of documents describing a fictional business problem of the kind you would meet in this role. Analyse them and produce a written report with your recommendation; expect questions on it afterwards. *(composed — in the style of the case study described by University of York Careers)* [16]
3. Analyse the attached qualitative and quantitative data and construct an argument for the option you recommend. *(composed — in the style of the case study described by University of Kent Careers)* [15]
4. You are an administrator asked to replace a colleague and deal with the situation described in this booklet, which sets out background on a forthcoming accession and the preparatory measures required; produce recommendations on the major problems and a schedule for the IT project. *(sourced: EPSO, "Assignment for the Situational Competency Based Interview", open competitions EPSO/AD/365-370/19 — note the competency labels in this 2020 booklet predate EPSO's 2023 framework)* [7]
5. Write a one-page briefing note for a minister who has ten minutes to read it, setting out the two options and your recommendation. *(composed — in the style of the Civil Service "written analysis/exercise" simulation)* [1]
6. Your recommendation has been challenged on cost grounds since you submitted it. Write a 150-word response. *(composed — written follow-up probe)*

## Presentations — prepared and unseen

1. Deliver a 5-minute presentation covering a set topic related to your leadership experience. *(sourced: Cabinet Office, "Application of Success Profiles during HR Director recruitment", April 2020)* [5]
2. Prepare for a topic discussion of approximately 30 minutes by researching and conducting analysis on the topic we have sent you; the discussion will be two-way, with the assessor asking questions. *(sourced: Deloitte UK, "Early Careers Assessment Support" — final-stage topic discussion)* [18]
3. You have 20 minutes and the materials in this pack. Present your recommendation to the panel for 10 minutes, then take questions. *(composed — unseen/on-the-spot presentation in the style of the design described by University of Kent Careers)* [15]
4. Present, in five minutes, the change you would make first if you took this role on Monday, and how you would know it had worked. *(composed)*
5. Your presentation is scheduled for eight minutes but the panel stops you at four and asks you to summarise. Do so. *(composed — time-management probe documented as an assessed dimension)* [16]
6. Present your team's group-exercise recommendation on the group's behalf, and be prepared to say which parts were not yours. *(composed — link between group exercise and presentation)*

## Role-plays and simulations

1. You are meeting a colleague whose recent conduct toward a team member has been reported to you. Read the brief; you have 10 minutes to prepare and 15 minutes with the role actor. *(composed — in the style of the diversity-topic role-play design published by the College of Policing)* [4]
2. You are the account manager. The client in the next room has just been told their delivery date has slipped by six weeks. *(composed — customer-facing role-play as described by University of Kent Careers)* [15]
3. A member of the public approaches you distressed about anti-social behaviour on their estate and wants immediate action. Respond. *(composed — in the style of the community-engagement work sample cited by the College of Policing)* [4]
4. You are a police constable dealing with the issues presented to you.
